---
title: "Banded Dotterel Post-breeding Moult"
date: "`r format(Sys.time(), '%H:%M %d %B, %Y')`"
author: 
 - name: Luke Eberhart-Hertel
   orcid: 0000-0001-7311-6088
   email: luke.eberhart@bi.mpg.de
   url: https://www.bi.mpg.de/person/115852/2867
   affiliations:
     - ref: bk
 - name: Bashar Jarayseh
   affiliations:
     - ref: bk
 - name: Ailsa Howard
   affiliations:
     - ref: ah
 - name: Ted Howard
   affiliations:
     - ref: ah
 - name: Tony Habraken
   affiliations:
     - ref: th
 - name: Emma Williams
   affiliations:
     - ref: ew
 - name: Colin O`Donnell
   affiliations:
     - ref: ew
 - name: Bart Kempenaers
   affiliations:
     - ref: bk
affiliations:
 - id: bk
   number: 1
   name: Department of Ornithology, Max Planck Institute for Biological Intelligence, Seewiesen, Germany
 - id: ah
   number: 2
   name: South Bay Banded Dotterel Project, Kaikoura, New Zealand
 - id: th
   number: 2
   name: Port Waikato Banded Dotterel Project, Port Waikato, New Zealand
 - id: ew
   number: 3
   name: Department of Conservation, Christchurch, New Zealand
format: 
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: show
    code-summary: "Show code"
    code-tools: true
    html-math-method: katex
    self-contained: true
    highlight-style: github
    theme: Cosmo
execute:
  warning: false
  cache: true
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

```{r, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(cache = TRUE)
options(repos = c(CRAN = "https://cran.rstudio.com"))
```

```{r, message=FALSE, results='hide', warning=FALSE, cache=FALSE, include=FALSE}
## Prerequisites

# cmdstanr needs to be installed and set-up with the following code:
# install.packages("cmdstanr", repos = c('https://stan-dev.r-universe.dev', getOption("repos")))
# cmdstanr::install_cmdstan()

### R packages
# -   The following packages are needed for analysis and can be easily installed from [CRAN](http://cran.r-project.org/) or GitHub by running the following code chunk:

# a vector of all the packages needed in the project
packages_required_in_project <- c("tidyverse",
                                  "readxl",
                                  "RMark",
                                  "RColorBrewer",
                                  "patchwork",
                                  "mapview",
                                  "lubridate",
                                  "extrafont",
                                  "here",
                                  "DT",
                                  "leaflet",
                                  "sf",
                                  "leafpop",
                                  "tsibble",
                                  "corrplot",
                                  "gghalves",
                                  "gam",
                                  "pscl",
                                  "gamlss",
                                  "gt",
                                  "lme4",
                                  "ggpattern",
                                  "gtsummary",
                                  "effects",
                                  "lattice",
                                  "rptR",
                                  "partR2",
                                  "broom.mixed",
                                  "forcats",
                                  "glmmTMB",
                                  "sysfonts",
                                  "showtext",
                                  "patchwork", "standardize",
                                  "gridExtra", "nlme", "ggridges", "brms", "cmdstanr", "tidybayes", "performance", "loo")

# of the required packages, check if some need to be installed
new.packages <- 
  packages_required_in_project[!(packages_required_in_project %in% 
                                   installed.packages()[,"Package"])]

# install all packages that are not locally available
if(length(new.packages)) install.packages(new.packages)

# load all the packages into the current R session
lapply(packages_required_in_project, require, character.only = TRUE)

# set the home directory to where the project is locally based (i.e., to find 
# the relevant datasets to import, etc.
here::set_here()

# set up cmdstanr to operate of your machine
cmdstanr::set_cmdstan_path()
cmdstanr::cmdstan_path()
cmdstanr::check_cmdstan_toolchain()
```

```{r, message=FALSE, results='hide', warning=FALSE, include=FALSE}
# Add Google font Verdana
font_add_google("Lato", "Lato")
font_add_google("Noto Sans", "NotoSans")

# define the plotting theme to be used in subsequent ggplots
luke_theme <- 
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(family = "Lato"),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.x  = element_text(size = 8), 
    axis.title.y = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8),
    strip.text = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_line(linewidth = 0.5, colour = "grey40"),
    axis.ticks.length = unit(0.2, "cm"),
    panel.border = element_rect(linetype = "solid", colour = "grey"),
    legend.position.inside = c(0.1, 0.9)
  )
```

```{r, include=FALSE}
# Define a custom mode function
get_mode <- function(x) {
  uniq_vals <- unique(x)
  uniq_vals[which.max(tabulate(match(x, uniq_vals)))]
}
```

## Data import and sample summary

We used repeated photographs from an individually marked breeding population at Kaikoura to ask how the expression of the rufous breast band and the timing of its post-breeding molt varies with sex, age, migratory strategy, and breeding phenology. Our full dataset spanned three breeding seasons and was linked to an individual's breeding records and (if known) their age and migratory status. This section loads the full dataset used throughout the markdown and gives a brief summary of seasonal coverage and sample size before proceeding to the hypotheses and predictions, analysis, and results.

```{r}
dat_breeding <- 
  readRDS(
    here("data/moult_breeding_KK_data.rds")
  )
```

We first summarise the number of photographs and the date range covered
in each season.

```{r, echo= FALSE}
dat_breeding %>% 
  group_by(season) %>% 
  summarise(n_obs = n(),
            min_date = min(date),
            max_date = max(date))
```

We then count the number of individuals represented in the full
photographic dataset.
```{r, echo = FALSE}
dat_breeding %>%
  pull(rings_comb) %>% unique() %>% length()
```

Finally, we check how many individuals were sampled in more than one
season, because repeated sampling across years is what allows us to
estimate persistent individual differences.
```{r, echo = FALSE}
dat_breeding %>%
  # subset to columns of interest
  select(score, date_J, rings_comb, sex, season) %>%
  # reduce to distinct data
  distinct() %>% 
  # scale the date_J variable
  group_by(season) %>% 
  mutate(date_J_scaled = as.vector(scale(date_J))) %>% 
  ungroup() %>%
  # drop NAs
  drop_na(score, date_J, sex, rings_comb, season) %>% 
  # convert to dataframe
  as.data.frame() %>%
  select(rings_comb, season) %>% 
  distinct() %>% 
  group_by(rings_comb) %>% 
  summarise(n_seasons = n()) %>% 
  group_by(n_seasons) %>% 
  summarise(n())
```

total, mean, and SD of photos for each individual per season
```{r, echo=FALSE}
dat_breeding %>%
  # subset to columns of interest
  select(score, date_J, rings_comb, sex, season) %>%
  # reduce to distinct data
  distinct() %>%
  # scale the date_J variable
  group_by(season) %>% 
  mutate(date_J_scaled = as.vector(scale(date_J))) %>% 
  ungroup() %>%
  # drop NAs
  drop_na(score, date_J, sex, rings_comb, season) %>% 
  # convert to dataframe
  as.data.frame() %>% 
  group_by(rings_comb, season) %>% 
  summarise(n_photos = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>% 
  mutate(total_photo = sum(n_photos),
         mean_photos_per_ind_per_season = mean(n_photos),
         sd_photos_per_ind_per_season = sd(n_photos)) %>% 
  distinct(total_photo, mean_photos_per_ind_per_season, sd_photos_per_ind_per_season)
```

max and min number of photos per individual per season
```{r, echo=FALSE}
dat_breeding %>%
  # subset to columns of interest
  select(score, date_J, rings_comb, sex, season) %>%
  # reduce to distinct data
  distinct() %>% 
  # scale the date_J variable
  group_by(season) %>% 
  mutate(date_J_scaled = as.vector(scale(date_J))) %>% 
  ungroup() %>%
  # drop NAs
  drop_na(score, date_J, sex, rings_comb, season) %>% 
  # convert to dataframe
  as.data.frame() %>% 
  group_by(rings_comb, season) %>% 
  summarise(n_photos = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>% 
  mutate(sum_n = sum(n_photos),
         mean_n = mean(n_photos),
         sd_n = sd(n_photos)) %>% 
  summarise(max_photos_ind_season = max(n_photos),
            min_photos_ind_season = min(n_photos))
```

### Table of scored photos
Note, in the analysed dataset, for a given individual, each date had only one score, hence the larger sample shown in this table. 

#### Variable definitions

- <code>sourcefile</code>: the directory path to the original photo scored from Ailsa's catalogue
- <code>rings_comb</code>: the 4-band color combination identifying an individual
- <code>score</code>: the score of the rufous breast band according to the 1-7 scheme shown below
- <code>date</code>: the date of the photo (extracted from the photo's metadata)
- <code>date_J</code>: the Julian date of the photo, shifted to have an July 1 origin for the autral summer
- <code>sex</code>: sex based on plumage (M or F)
- <code>season</code>: breeding season of the given photo
- <code>migratory_status</code>: Resident (R) or migrant (M) classified from satellite tracking and/or winter resightings
- <code>age</code>: known age in years at the time of the photo (only available for those that were banded locally as chicks and recruited)

```{r, echo=FALSE}
dat_breeding %>%
  select(sourcefile, rings_comb, score, date, date_J, sex, season, migratory_status, age) %>% 
  datatable(class = 'cell-border stripe', rownames = FALSE, filter = 'top')
```

### Summary table of usable photos for each individual across the three seasons
```{r, echo=FALSE}
dat_breeding %>% 
  select(sourcefile, rings_comb, score, date_J, sex, season, migratory_status, age) %>% 
  distinct(rings_comb, score, date_J, sex, season, migratory_status, age) %>%
  group_by(rings_comb, season) %>% 
  summarise(n_photos = n()) %>% 
  arrange(desc(n_photos)) %>% 
  pivot_wider(., names_from = season, values_from = n_photos) %>% 
  datatable(class = 'cell-border stripe', rownames = FALSE, filter = 'top')
```

### Scoring scheme

The "score" value is based on a scale from 1 to 7 describing the immaculate-ness and full-ness of the rufous breast band seen in the photos

```{r, echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics("/Users/luketheduke2/ownCloud/kemp_projects/bdot/moult/figs/combined_figure_draft.png")
```

```{r, include=FALSE}
dat_breeding %>% 
  select(sex, rings_comb, season, score, date) %>% 
  group_by(sex, rings_comb, season) %>% 
  summarise(n_unique_scores = n_distinct(score)) %>% 
  arrange(desc(n_unique_scores))
```

### Sampling distribution of Ailsa's photos across the seasons

#### 2021-2022 season data

```{r, fig.height=9, echo=FALSE}
# 2021-2022 season data
female_2021 <- ggplot(data = dat_breeding %>% 
                        filter(season == 2021 & sex == "F")) +
  geom_point(aes(y = 1, x = date, fill = as.numeric(score)), 
             pch = 21, color = "black", size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(. ~ reorder(rings_comb, n_photos, decreasing = TRUE), 
             ncol = 1, strip.position = "right") +
  scale_fill_gradient(high = "#cc4c02", low = "white") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.title.y = element_blank(),
        axis.text.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        strip.text.y.right = element_text(angle = 0),
    panel.border = element_rect(linetype = "solid", colour = "grey90"),
    strip.background = element_rect(color = "grey90", fill = "grey90"),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_line(color = "grey30")) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%W", expand = c(0.01, 0.01), 
               date_breaks = "3 week", 
               limits = c(as.Date("2021-07-05"), as.Date("2022-05-01"))) +
  xlab("week") +
  ggtitle("Females from the 2021-2022 breeding season in Kaikoura")

female_2021
```

```{r, fig.height=8, echo=FALSE}
male_2021 <- ggplot(data = dat_breeding %>% 
                      filter(season == 2021 & sex == "M")) +
  geom_point(aes(y = 1, x = date, fill = as.numeric(score)), 
             pch = 21, color = "black", size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(. ~ reorder(rings_comb, n_photos, decreasing = TRUE), 
             ncol = 1, strip.position = "right") +
  scale_fill_gradient(high = "#cc4c02", low = "white") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.title.y = element_blank(),
        axis.text.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        strip.text.y.right = element_text(angle = 0),
    panel.border = element_rect(linetype = "solid", colour = "grey90"),
    strip.background = element_rect(color = "grey90", fill = "grey90"),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_line(color = "grey30")) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%W", expand = c(0.01, 0.01), 
               date_breaks = "3 week", 
               limits = c(as.Date("2021-07-05"), as.Date("2022-05-01"))) +
  xlab("week") +
  ggtitle("Males from the 2021-2022 breeding season in Kaikoura")

male_2021
```

#### 2022-2023 season data

```{r, fig.height=10, echo=FALSE}
# 2022-2023 season data (not as good coverage as the previous season)
female_2022 <- ggplot(data = dat_breeding %>% 
                        filter(season == 2022 & sex == "F")) +
  geom_point(aes(y = 1, x = date, fill = as.numeric(score)), 
             pch = 21, color = "black", size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(. ~ reorder(rings_comb, n_photos, decreasing = TRUE), 
             ncol = 1, strip.position = "right") +
  scale_fill_gradient(high = "#cc4c02", low = "white") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.title.y = element_blank(),
        axis.text.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        strip.text.y.right = element_text(angle = 0),
    panel.border = element_rect(linetype = "solid", colour = "grey90"),
    strip.background = element_rect(color = "grey90", fill = "grey90"),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_line(color = "grey30")) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%W", expand = c(0.01, 0.01), 
               date_breaks = "3 week", 
               limits = c(as.Date("2022-07-05"), as.Date("2023-05-01"))) +
  xlab("week") +
  ggtitle("Females from the 2022-2023 breeding season in Kaikoura")

female_2022

```

```{r, fig.height=7, echo=FALSE}
male_2022 <- ggplot(data = dat_breeding %>% 
                      filter(season == 2022 & sex == "M")) +
  geom_point(aes(y = 1, x = date, fill = as.numeric(score)), 
             pch = 21, color = "black", size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(. ~ reorder(rings_comb, n_photos, decreasing = TRUE), 
             ncol = 1, strip.position = "right") +
  scale_fill_gradient(high = "#cc4c02", low = "white") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.title.y = element_blank(),
        axis.text.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        strip.text.y.right = element_text(angle = 0),
    panel.border = element_rect(linetype = "solid", colour = "grey90"),
    strip.background = element_rect(color = "grey90", fill = "grey90"),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_line(color = "grey30")) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%W", expand = c(0.01, 0.01), 
               date_breaks = "3 week", 
               limits = c(as.Date("2022-07-05"), as.Date("2023-05-01"))) +
  xlab("week") +
  ggtitle("Males from the 2022-2023 breeding season in Kaikoura")

male_2022

```

#### 2023-2024 season data
```{r, fig.height=10, echo=FALSE}
# 2023-2024 season data 
female_2023 <- ggplot(data = dat_breeding %>% 
                        filter(season == 2023 & sex == "F")) +
  geom_point(aes(y = 1, x = date, fill = as.numeric(score)), 
             pch = 21, color = "black", size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(. ~ reorder(rings_comb, n_photos, decreasing = TRUE), 
             ncol = 1, strip.position = "right") +
  scale_fill_gradient(high = "#cc4c02", low = "white") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.title.y = element_blank(),
        axis.text.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        strip.text.y.right = element_text(angle = 0),
    panel.border = element_rect(linetype = "solid", colour = "grey90"),
    strip.background = element_rect(color = "grey90", fill = "grey90"),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_line(color = "grey30")) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%W", expand = c(0.01, 0.01), 
               date_breaks = "3 week", 
               limits = c(as.Date("2023-07-01"), as.Date("2024-05-01"))) +
  xlab("week") +
  ggtitle("Females from the 2023-2024 breeding season in Kaikoura")

female_2023
```

```{r, fig.height=6, echo=FALSE}
male_2023 <- ggplot(data = dat_breeding %>% 
                      filter(season == 2023 & sex == "M")) +
  geom_point(aes(y = 1, x = date, fill = as.numeric(score)), 
             pch = 21, color = "black", size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(. ~ reorder(rings_comb, n_photos, decreasing = TRUE), 
             ncol = 1, strip.position = "right") +
  scale_fill_gradient(high = "#cc4c02", low = "white") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.title.y = element_blank(),
        axis.text.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        strip.text.y.right = element_text(angle = 0),
    panel.border = element_rect(linetype = "solid", colour = "grey90"),
    strip.background = element_rect(color = "grey90", fill = "grey90"),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_line(color = "grey30")) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%W", expand = c(0.01, 0.01), 
               date_breaks = "3 week", 
               limits = c(as.Date("2023-07-05"), as.Date("2024-05-01"))) +
  xlab("week") +
  ggtitle("Males from the 2023-2024 breeding season in Kaikoura")

male_2023
```

## Hypotheses and predictions
We tested the following five hypotheses. The 'Sex' and 'Breeding phenology' models used the full dataset, while the 'Age class', 'Continuous age', and 'Migratory status' models used subsets of the full dataset for individuals of known age or migratory status (see Table 1 for sample sizes).

### Sex
- H1: sexual dichromatism in the rufous band score and sex-specific molt timing
- P1a: males tend to have higher rufous band scores than females, following the general patter of moderate sexual dichromatism in other Charadriidae plovers
- P1b: no difference in post-breeding molt timing due to the socially monogamous and bi-parental care mating system

### Breeding phenology
- H2: post-breeding molt is dependent upon breeding phenology
- P2a: individuals that breed earlier molt earlier due a generally more advanced reproductive schedule
- P2b: individuals that are still engaged in parental care late in the season molt later
- P2c: individuals that initiate late nests molt later

### Age class
- H3: age-class structures the molt schedule and rufous band expression
- P3a: first-year birds express lower rufous band scores in comparison to after-first-year birds due to more complete pigmentation during feather growth in older birds
- P3b: first-year birds molt earlier than after-first-year birds because the flight feathers of first-years are older (and more worn) than after-first-years, which triggers an earlier complete post-breeding molt program

### Continuous age
- H4: beyond the first-year transition continuous age structures the molt schedule and rufous band expression
- P4a: no age-dependent variation in rufous band expression (either within or between individuals)
- P4b: no age-dependent variation in post-breeding molt timing (either within or between individuals)

### Migratory strategy 
- H5: migratory strategy structures the molt schedule and rufous band expression
- P5a: no strategy-dependent variation in rufous band expression
- P5b: migrants molt earlier than residents due to their more constrained fall migration schedule

## Non-linear mixed effects model

We scored the rufous breast band on a seven-point scale, so the response is bounded and declines through the season as birds transition from their breeding ("alternate") plumage into their non-breeding plumage ("basic") via the complete post-breeding molt. 

We fit the same nonlinear trajectory for all five models and let the predictors explain variation in the starting score and the timing of the decline.

We modeled the decline in rufous band score with a nonlinear mixed-effects curve. Because we centered and scaled Julian date within season before fitting, we write the seasonal date term as $x$. For observation $j$ on bird $i$,

$$
y_{ij} \sim \mathrm{Normal}(\mu_{ij}, \sigma).
$$

$$
\mu_{ij} =
1 + (\beta_{ij} - 1)
\left(
1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp[-k(x_{ij} - t_{0,ij})]}
\right).
$$

The parameter $\beta$ is the rufous band score prior to the post-breeding molt, $t_0$ is the midpoint of the decline to the non-breeding plumage, and $k$ controls how steep/fast the molt progresses. Early in the season ($x \ll t_0$), expected scores are close to $\beta$; late in the season ($x \gg t_0$), expected scores approach 1 (i.e. full non-breeding plumage). This function attempts to match the biology of post-breeding moult, in which birds move from full breeding plumage toward full non-breeding plumage over time.

In the sex-specific model, we allowed both $\beta$ and $t_0$ to depend on sex and to vary among birds and bird-by-season combinations, while keeping $k$ common across birds (i.e., our photographic sampling was too coarse to robustly assess sources of variation in molt speed):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta_{ij} &= \alpha_{\beta} + \mathbf{X}_{ij}\boldsymbol{\gamma}_{\beta} + b_{\beta,i} + b_{\beta,is}, \\
t_{0,ij} &= \alpha_{t_0} + \mathbf{X}_{ij}\boldsymbol{\gamma}_{t_0} + b_{t_0,i} + b_{t_0,is}, \\
k &= \alpha_k .
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, $\mathbf{X}$ denotes the relevant fixed effects for a given model. In the sex-only model, $\mathbf{X}$ contains sex; in the other models, we added breeding phenology, age class, continuous age terms (i.e., within- vs. between-individual), or migratory status. We kept the same nonlinear form throughout so that differences among models reflect changes in predictors of $\beta$ and $t_0$, not changes in the underlying molt curve.

We used an informative prior on $\beta$ (i.e., <code>normal(5.5, 1)</code>; centering the prior near the upper half of the 1-7 scoring scale and keeping it positive), weakly informative priors on $t_0$ and $k$, and exponential priors on the group-level standard deviations. This combination stabilized estimation while still allowing substantial variation among birds and among bird-by-season combinations when the data supported it.

The Figure 1 schematic below shows how $\beta$, $t_0$, and $k$ shape the curve that we fit in every model. We use representative values here so that the roles of the three parameters are easy to interpret.

### Figure 1 - schematic of the non-linear model
```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code of an example parameterizing the non-linear model"

# Draw a schematic version of the nonlinear curve used in all models.
# The values of beta, t0, and k are chosen for illustration only.

# Set the schematic parameters.
baseline <- 6
k <- 3.31
t0 <- 1.45

# Define the expected trajectory from the nonlinear model.
moult_mu <- function(x, baseline, k, t0) {
  1 + (baseline - 1) * (1 - 1 / (1 + exp(-k * (x - t0))))
}

# Build a clean x-range around the inflection point.
x <- seq(t0 - 2.5, t0 + 2.5, length.out = 600)

df <- tibble(
  x = x,
  mu = moult_mu(x, baseline = baseline, k = k, t0 = t0)
)

# At x = t0 the curve is halfway between the upper and lower asymptotes.
y_t0 <- moult_mu(t0, baseline = baseline, k = k, t0 = t0)

# The slope at t0 determines the tangent line used to illustrate k.
slope_t0 <- -(baseline - 1) * k / 4
angle_k <- atan(slope_t0) * 180 / pi

y_k <- 7
x_k <- t0 + (y_k - y_t0) / slope_t0
```

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show plotting code for the example"
fig1 <- 
  ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = mu)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = baseline, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1, color = "grey70") +
  ggtext::geom_richtext(
    data = data.frame(x = min(df$x), y = baseline),
    aes(x = x, y = y - 0.1),
    label = "baseline (<span style='font-family:NotoSans; font-style:italic;'>β</span>)",
    family = "Lato", size = 3,
    hjust = 0, vjust = -0.6,
    fill = NA, label.color = NA
  ) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = t0, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1, color = "grey70") +
  geom_point(aes(x = t0, y = y_t0), size = 2) +
  annotate(
    "text", size = 3,
    family = "Lato",
    x = t0, y = min(df$mu),
    label = as.expression(bquote("molt midpoint" ~ "(" * italic(t)[0] * ")" == .(round(t0, 2)))),
    parse = FALSE,
    hjust = 1, vjust = 1.5, angle = 270
  ) +
  {
    slope_t0 <- -(baseline - 1) * k / 4
    geom_abline(
      intercept = y_t0 - slope_t0 * t0,
      slope = slope_t0,
      linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1, color = "grey70"
    )
  } +
  annotate(
    "text", size = 3,
    family = "Lato",
    x = x_k, y = y_k,
    label = as.expression(bquote("molt rate (" * italic(k) * ") = " * .(round(k, 2)))),
    hjust = 0.5,
    vjust = -0.5,
    angle = angle_k + 5
  ) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 2, color = "grey30") +
  geom_point(aes(x = t0, y = y_t0), size = 5, shape = 21, fill = "white", color = "black") +
  geom_point(aes(x = -0.5, y = 6), size = 5, shape = 23, fill = "white", color = "black") +
  labs(
    x = "Scaled date (mean-centered Julian date)",
    y = "Rufous band score"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(1, 8)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(1, 8), breaks = c(1:7)) +
  theme(
    text = element_text(family = "Lato"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_text(color = "grey30")
  )

fig1
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
ggsave(
  fig1,
  filename = "fig1_schematic_plot.pdf",
  path = here("figs/"),
  device = cairo_pdf,
  width = 5, height = 5
)
```

### Shared priors

We use the same prior set in all five non-linear moult models so that
differences among models reflect changes in predictors rather than
changes in prior assumptions. We define that shared prior set once here
and reuse it in each `brm()` call below.

```{r}
# Shared priors for all non-linear moult models.
moult_priors <- c(
  # The baseline score sits on the 1-7 scoring scale, so we center its
  # prior near the upper half of that range while keeping it positive.
  prior(normal(5.5, 1), nlpar = "baseline", lb = 0),

  # The inflection point is estimated on the season-standardized date scale.
  prior(normal(0, 0.5), nlpar = "t0"),

  # Positive values of k enforce the expected one-way seasonal decline.
  prior(normal(1, 0.5), nlpar = "k", lb = 0),

  # Group-level SDs are regularized, but can still be large if the data
  # support substantial heterogeneity among birds or bird-seasons.
  prior(exponential(2), nlpar = "baseline", class = "sd", group = "rings_comb"),
  prior(exponential(2), nlpar = "baseline", class = "sd", group = "rings_comb:season"),
  prior(exponential(2), nlpar = "t0", class = "sd", group = "rings_comb"),
  prior(exponential(2), nlpar = "t0", class = "sd", group = "rings_comb:season")
)
```

### Analysis: Sex-specific model
We first fit a model to the full dataset and asked whether males and females differed in $\beta$ and $t_0$. We used this as the reference model because sex was known for all birds and it gives the cleanest estimate of the post-breeding molt trajectory and baseline expression of the rufous band.

#### dataset
```{r}
# Prepare data
dat_sex_mod <- dat_breeding %>%
  # subset to columns of interest
  select(score, date_J, rings_comb, sex, season) %>%
  # reduce to distinct data
  distinct() %>% 
  # scale the date_J variable
  group_by(season) %>% 
  mutate(date_J_scaled = as.vector(scale(date_J))) %>% 
  ungroup() %>%
  # drop NAs
  drop_na(score, date_J, sex, rings_comb, season) %>% 
  # convert to dataframe
  as.data.frame()
```

#### model formula specification
```{r}
mod_sex <- bf(
  score ~ 1 + (baseline - 1) * (1 - 1 / (1 + exp(-k * (date_J_scaled - t0)))),
  baseline ~ sex + (1 | i | rings_comb) + (1 | rings_comb:season),
  t0 ~ sex + (1 | i | rings_comb) + (1 | rings_comb:season),
  k ~ 1,
  nl = TRUE
)
```

#### fit model
```{r, eval=FALSE}
fit_mod_sex <- brm(
  formula = mod_sex,
  data = dat_sex_mod,
  family = gaussian(),
  prior = moult_priors,
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.999, max_treedepth = 15),
  chains = 4,
  cores = 4,
  iter = 10000,
  warmup = 5000,
  backend = "cmdstanr",
  seed = 123
)
```

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
saveRDS(list(fitted_model = fit_mod_sex, data = dat_sex_mod),
        file = here("out/sex_mod.rds"))
```

### Analysis: Breeding Phenology model
We then retained sex in the model and added first breeding date, last breeding date, and last nest initiation date as predictors of molt timing ($t_0$). Any carry-over effect of breeding should appear first in the timing of the decline, so here we focus on whether later breeding activity shifts the molt midpoint.

#### datasets
here we wrangle three datasets. The first (<code>dat_breeding_mod</code>) uses the entire dataset and is what is presented in the paper. The second (<code>dat_breeding_mod2</code>) is a more conservative dataset that subsets to only include individuals that were photographed after the last breeding date (i.e., this was an attempt to focus the estimation of the molt mid-point parameter on birds that had their molt scored after known breeding cessation). The third (<code>dat_breeding_mod3</code>) is another conservative dataset that subsets to include only individuals that had their last known breeding attempt occur after the population's median for a given season (i.e., this was an attempt to remove outlier birds that may have dispersed after an early breeding attempt and were not seen again later in the season when most of the other birds had their last breeding attempt). All three datasets show the same effect, and hence only the results from the full dataset is presented.
```{r}
# wrangle data
dat_breeding_mod <- dat_breeding %>%
  # subset to columns of interest
  select(score, date_J, rings_comb, sex, season, 
         last_breeding_date_J_snum, 
         first_breeding_date_J_snum,
         last_nest_first_obs_J_snum) %>% 
  # reduce to distinct data
  distinct() %>% 
  # scale the date_J variable
  group_by(season) %>% 
  mutate(date_J_scaled = as.vector(scale(date_J))) %>% 
  ungroup() %>%
  # drop NAs
  drop_na(score, date_J, sex, rings_comb, season, 
          last_breeding_date_J_snum, 
          first_breeding_date_J_snum, 
          last_nest_first_obs_J_snum) %>% 
  # convert to dataframe
  as.data.frame()

# subset to include only birds that were photographed after their last breeding activity
dat_breeding2 <- 
  dat_breeding %>%
  group_by(rings_comb, season) %>%
  mutate(
    max_date = max(date_J, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  filter(last_breeding_date_J < max_date)

# wrangle data and subset to dat_breeding2
dat_breeding_mod2 <- dat_breeding2 %>%
  # subset to columns of interest
  select(score, date_J, rings_comb, sex, season, 
         last_breeding_date_J_snum, 
         first_breeding_date_J_snum,
         last_nest_first_obs_J_snum) %>%
  # reduce to distinct data
  distinct() %>% 
  # scale the date_J variable
  group_by(season) %>% 
  mutate(date_J_scaled = as.vector(scale(date_J))) %>% 
  ungroup() %>%
  # drop NAs
  drop_na(score, date_J, sex, rings_comb, season, 
          last_breeding_date_J_snum, 
          first_breeding_date_J_snum, 
          last_nest_first_obs_J_snum) %>% 
  # convert to dataframe
  as.data.frame()

# wrangle data and subset to individuals that had late last breeding dates
dat_breeding_mod3 <- dat_breeding %>%
  # remove individuals that had early last breeding dates (i.e., likely dispersed and were not seen again)
  filter(last_breeding_date_J_snum > 0) %>% 
  # subset to columns of interest
  select(score, date_J, rings_comb, sex, season, 
         last_breeding_date_J_snum, 
         first_breeding_date_J_snum,
         last_nest_first_obs_J_snum) %>%
  # reduce to distinct data
  distinct() %>% 
  # scale the date_J variable
  group_by(season) %>% 
  mutate(date_J_scaled = as.vector(scale(date_J))) %>% 
  ungroup() %>%
  # drop NAs
  drop_na(score, date_J, sex, rings_comb, season, 
          last_breeding_date_J_snum, 
          first_breeding_date_J_snum, 
          last_nest_first_obs_J_snum) %>% 
  # convert to dataframe
  as.data.frame()
```

#### model formula specification
```{r}
mod_sex_all_3_breed <- bf(
   score ~ 1 + (baseline - 1) * (1 - 1 / (1 + exp(-k * (date_J_scaled - t0)))),
   baseline ~ sex + (1 | i | rings_comb) + (1 | rings_comb:season),
   t0 ~ sex + first_breeding_date_J_snum + last_nest_first_obs_J_snum + last_breeding_date_J_snum + (1 | i | rings_comb) + (1 | rings_comb:season),
   k ~ 1,
   nl = TRUE
)
```

#### fit models
Following the three datasets described above, we evaluated three versions of the breeding phenology model. Results were the same for all three datasets/models, and so only the complete dataset/model is presented in the paper
```{r, eval=FALSE}
fit_mod_sex_all_3_breed <- brm(
  formula = mod_sex_all_3_breed,
  data = dat_breeding_mod,
  family = gaussian(),
  prior = moult_priors,
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.999, max_treedepth = 15),
  chains = 4,
  cores = 4,
  iter = 10000,
  warmup = 5000,
  backend = "cmdstanr",
  seed = 123
)

fit_mod_sex_all_3_breed2 <- brm(
  formula = mod_sex_all_3_breed,
  data = dat_breeding_mod2,
  family = gaussian(),
  prior = moult_priors,
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.999, max_treedepth = 15),
  chains = 4,
  cores = 4,
  iter = 10000,
  warmup = 5000,
  backend = "cmdstanr",
  seed = 123
)

fit_mod_sex_all_3_breed3 <- brm(
  formula = mod_sex_all_3_breed,
  data = dat_breeding_mod3,
  family = gaussian(),
  prior = moult_priors,
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.999, max_treedepth = 15),
  chains = 4,
  cores = 4,
  iter = 10000,
  warmup = 5000,
  backend = "cmdstanr",
  seed = 123
)
```

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
saveRDS(list(fitted_model = fit_mod_sex_all_3_breed, data = dat_breeding_mod), 
        file = here("out/sex_all_3_breed_mod.rds"))

saveRDS(list(fitted_model = fit_mod_sex_all_3_breed2, data = dat_breeding_mod2), 
        file = here("out/sex_all_3_breed_mod2.rds"))

saveRDS(list(fitted_model = fit_mod_sex_all_3_breed3, data = dat_breeding_mod3), 
        file = here("out/sex_all_3_breed_mod3.rds"))
```

### Analysis: Age Class model
We next incorporated age class (first-year versus after-first-year) to the sex-specific model to test on a subser of known aged birds whether individuals in the first molt cycle differ from after-first-year birds in baseline plumage and molt timing.

#### dataset
```{r}
# wrangle data
dat_age_class_mod <- dat_breeding %>%
  # subset to columns of interest
  select(score, date_J, rings_comb, sex, season, age_class) %>%
  # reduce to distinct data
  distinct() %>%
  # scale the date_J variable
  group_by(season) %>% 
  mutate(date_J_scaled = as.vector(scale(date_J))) %>% 
  ungroup() %>%
  # drop NAs
  drop_na(score, date_J, sex, rings_comb, season, age_class) %>% 
  # convert to dataframe
  as.data.frame()
```

#### model formula specification
```{r}
mod_sex_age_class <- bf(
  score ~ 1 + (baseline - 1) * (1 - 1 / (1 + exp(-k * (date_J_scaled - t0)))),
  baseline ~ sex + age_class + (1 | i | rings_comb) + (1 | rings_comb:season),
  t0 ~ sex + age_class + (1 | i | rings_comb) + (1 | rings_comb:season),
  k ~ 1,
  nl = TRUE
)
```

#### fit model
```{r, eval=FALSE}
fit_mod_sex_age_class <- brm(
  formula = mod_sex_age_class,
  data = dat_age_class_mod,
  family = gaussian(),
  prior = moult_priors,
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.999, max_treedepth = 15),
  chains = 4,
  cores = 4,
  iter = 10000,
  warmup = 5000,
  backend = "cmdstanr",
  save_pars = save_pars(all = TRUE),
  seed = 123
)
```

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
saveRDS(list(fitted_model = fit_mod_sex_age_class, 
             data = dat_age_class_mod),
        file = here("out/sex_age_class_mod.rds"))
```

### Analysis: Continuous Age model
Following Van de Pol & Verhulst (2006), we decomposed age ($x_{ist}$) into each bird's mean age across the three-year study period ($\bar{x}_i$; the between-individual effect, implemented as `avg_age`) and the deviation from that mean for a given observation ($x_{ist} - \bar{x}_i$; the within-individual effect, implemented as `age_dev`). This allowed us to test whether any age effect extended beyond the first-year transition from the complex molt cycle rather than simply reflecting a first-year contrast.

#### datasets
```{r}
# calculate average age in the dataset of individuals of known age
avg_ages <- 
  dat_breeding %>%
  distinct(rings_comb, age) %>% 
  filter(!is.na(age)) %>% 
  group_by(rings_comb) %>% 
  summarise(avg_age = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE))

# calculate average age in the dataset of individuals of known age, excluding age 1
avg_ages2 <- 
  dat_breeding %>%
  distinct(rings_comb, age) %>% 
  filter(!is.na(age)) %>% 
  filter(age > 1) %>% 
  group_by(rings_comb) %>% 
  summarise(avg_age = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE))

# wrangle data
dat_age_mod <- dat_breeding %>%
  # subset to columns of interest
  select(score, date_J, rings_comb, sex, season, age) %>%
  # reduce to distinct data
  distinct() %>% 
  # scale the date_J variable
  group_by(season) %>% 
  mutate(date_J_scaled = as.vector(scale(date_J))) %>%
  # center age
  filter(!is.na(age)) %>% 
  left_join(., avg_ages, by = "rings_comb") %>% 
  # age deviance
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(age_dev = age - avg_age) %>% 
  # drop NAs
  drop_na(score, date_J, sex, rings_comb, 
          season, avg_age, age_dev) %>% 
  # convert to dataframe
  as.data.frame()

# wrangle data
dat_age2_mod <- dat_breeding %>%
  # subset to columns of interest
  select(score, date_J, rings_comb, sex, season, age) %>%
  # reduce to distinct data
  distinct() %>% 
  # scale the date_J variable
  group_by(season) %>% 
  mutate(date_J_scaled = as.vector(scale(date_J))) %>%
  # center age
  filter(!is.na(age)) %>%
  filter(age > 1) %>% 
  left_join(., avg_ages2, by = "rings_comb") %>% 
  # age deviance
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(age_dev = age - avg_age) %>% 
  # drop NAs
  drop_na(score, date_J, sex, rings_comb, 
          season, avg_age, age_dev) %>% 
  # convert to dataframe
  as.data.frame()
```

#### model formula specification
```{r}
mod_sex_age_avg <- bf(
  score ~ 1 + (baseline - 1) * (1 - 1 / (1 + exp(-k * (date_J_scaled - t0)))),
  baseline ~ sex + age_dev + avg_age + (1 | i | rings_comb) + (1 | rings_comb:season),
  t0 ~ sex + age_dev + avg_age + (1 | i | rings_comb) + (1 | rings_comb:season),
  k ~ 1,
  nl = TRUE
)
```

#### fit models
```{r, eval=FALSE}
fit_mod_sex_age_avg <- brm(
  formula = mod_sex_age_avg,
  data = dat_age_mod,
  family = gaussian(),
  prior = moult_priors,
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.999, max_treedepth = 15),
  chains = 4,
  cores = 4,
  iter = 10000,
  warmup = 5000,
  backend = "cmdstanr",
  save_pars = save_pars(all = TRUE),
  seed = 123
)

fit_mod_sex_age2_avg <- brm(
  formula = mod_sex_age_avg,
  data = dat_age2_mod,
  family = gaussian(),
  prior = moult_priors,
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.999, max_treedepth = 15),
  chains = 4,
  cores = 4,
  iter = 10000,
  warmup = 5000,
  backend = "cmdstanr",
  save_pars = save_pars(all = TRUE),
  seed = 123
)
```

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
saveRDS(list(fitted_model = fit_mod_sex_age_avg, 
             data = dat_age_mod),
        file = here("out/sex_age_avg_mod.rds"))
```

### Analysis: Migratory Strategy model
We finally tested whether migratory strategy explained variation in
$\beta$ and $t_0$ after accounting for sex. If migration imposes tighter
late-season constraints, we would expect migrants to move through the
post-breeding transition earlier than residents.

#### datasets
```{r}
# wrangle data
dat_migratory_status_mod <- 
  dat_breeding %>%
  # subset to columns of interest
  select(score, date_J, rings_comb, sex, season, migratory_status) %>%
  # reduce to distinct data
  distinct() %>% 
  # scale the date_J variable
  group_by(season) %>% 
  mutate(date_J_scaled = as.vector(scale(date_J))) %>% 
  ungroup() %>%
  # drop NAs
  drop_na(score, date_J, sex, rings_comb, season, migratory_status) %>% 
  # convert to dataframe
  as.data.frame()
```

#### model formula specification
```{r}
mod_sex_migratory_status <- bf(
  score ~ 1 + (baseline - 1) * (1 - 1 / (1 + exp(-k * (date_J_scaled - t0)))),
  baseline ~ sex + migratory_status + (1 | i | rings_comb) + (1 | rings_comb:season),
  t0 ~ sex + migratory_status + (1 | i | rings_comb) + (1 | rings_comb:season),
  k ~ 1,
  nl = TRUE
)
```

#### fit model
```{r, eval=FALSE}
fit_mod_sex_migratory_status <- brm(
  formula = mod_sex_migratory_status,
  data = dat_migratory_status_mod,
  family = gaussian(),
  prior = moult_priors,
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.999, max_treedepth = 15),
  chains = 4,
  cores = 4,
  iter = 10000,
  warmup = 5000,
  backend = "cmdstanr",
  seed = 123
)
```

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
saveRDS(list(fitted_model = fit_mod_sex_migratory_status, 
             data = dat_migratory_status_mod),
        file = here("out/sex_migratory_status_mod.rds"))
```

## Results
Using 1,647 scored photographs from 100 birds across three seasons, we found that 1) baseline rufous band score and molt timing both showed persistent differences among birds, 2) age explained variation both in terms of the first-year vs. after-first year contrast, and in the continuous age effect, and 3) there was little evidence that breeding phenology or migratory strategy shifted the molt schedule. The results below start from the saved model objects in `out/`, so this section can be rerun without refitting the time-consuming Bayesian models above. Helper chunks are hidden in the HTML to keep the shared document readable, while the visible chunks show only the short code needed to reproduce the reported tables.

```{r results-helpers, include=FALSE}
# Summarise posterior draws as a median and 95% credible interval.
summarise_posterior <- function(x) {
  tibble(
    median = median(x, na.rm = TRUE),
    ci_lower = as.numeric(quantile(x, 0.025, na.rm = TRUE)),
    ci_upper = as.numeric(quantile(x, 0.975, na.rm = TRUE))
  )
}

# Format numeric intervals consistently across all reported tables.
format_numeric_interval <- function(summary_tbl, digits = 2) {
  paste0(
    round(summary_tbl$median, digits), " [",
    round(summary_tbl$ci_lower, digits), ", ",
    round(summary_tbl$ci_upper, digits), "]"
  )
}

# Convert shifted Julian dates back to the month-day labels used in the text tables.
shifted_julian_to_date <- function(x, origin_year = 2021) {
  as.Date(x - 182, origin = paste0(origin_year, "-01-01"))
}

format_shifted_julian_interval <- function(summary_tbl, digits = 2, origin_year = 2021) {
  paste0(
    format(shifted_julian_to_date(summary_tbl$median, origin_year), "%b-%d"), " [",
    format(shifted_julian_to_date(summary_tbl$ci_lower, origin_year), "%b-%d"), ", ",
    format(shifted_julian_to_date(summary_tbl$ci_upper, origin_year), "%b-%d"), "]"
  )
}

# Build a summary row once the estimate has been reduced to a point estimate and interval.
summary_row_from_stats <- function(parameter, type, parameter_group, stats,
                                   digits = 2,
                                   formatter = format_numeric_interval, ...) {
  tibble(
    type = type,
    parameter_ = parameter_group,
    parameter = parameter,
    estimate = stats$median,
    ci_lower = stats$ci_lower,
    ci_upper = stats$ci_upper,
    median_CI = formatter(stats, digits = digits, ...)
  )
}

# Convenience wrapper when we start from raw posterior draws.
summary_row <- function(parameter, type, parameter_group, draws,
                        digits = 2,
                        formatter = format_numeric_interval, ...) {
  summary_row_from_stats(
    parameter = parameter,
    type = type,
    parameter_group = parameter_group,
    stats = summarise_posterior(draws),
    digits = digits,
    formatter = formatter,
    ...
  )
}

# Recover the season-weighted centering/scaling constants used to back-transform t0.
weighted_date_scaling <- function(data, date_col = "date_J") {
  date_stats <- data %>%
    group_by(season) %>%
    summarise(
      mean_value = mean(.data[[date_col]], na.rm = TRUE),
      sd_value = sd(.data[[date_col]], na.rm = TRUE),
      n = dplyr::n(),
      .groups = "drop"
    )

  tibble(
    weighted_mean = weighted.mean(date_stats$mean_value, w = date_stats$n, na.rm = TRUE),
    weighted_sd = weighted.mean(date_stats$sd_value, w = date_stats$n, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
}

# Recover a season-weighted SD for any breeding predictor on the original day scale.
weighted_predictor_sd <- function(data, value_col) {
  predictor_stats <- data %>%
    group_by(season) %>%
    summarise(
      sd_value = sd(.data[[value_col]], na.rm = TRUE),
      n = dplyr::n(),
      .groups = "drop"
    )

  weighted.mean(predictor_stats$sd_value, w = predictor_stats$n, na.rm = TRUE)
}

# Convert `performance::r2_bayes()` output into the same table-ready format used elsewhere.
summarise_bayes_r2 <- function(fitted_model) {
  r2_df <- performance::r2_bayes(fitted_model) %>% as.data.frame()

  tibble(
    parameter = c("Marginal R²", "Conditional R²"),
    median = c(r2_df$R2[2], r2_df$R2[1]),
    ci_lower = c(r2_df$CI_low[2], r2_df$CI_low[1]),
    ci_upper = c(r2_df$CI_high[2], r2_df$CI_high[1])
  )
}

# Add repeatability- and variance-related draws used in the sex-only summary table.
add_repeatability_draws <- function(post) {
  post %>%
    mutate(
      var_baseline_indiv =
        sd_rings_comb__baseline_Intercept^2 +
        `sd_rings_comb:season__baseline_Intercept`^2,
      var_t0_indiv =
        sd_rings_comb__t0_Intercept^2 +
        `sd_rings_comb:season__t0_Intercept`^2,
      R_baseline = var_baseline_indiv / (var_baseline_indiv + sigma^2),
      R_t0 = var_t0_indiv / (var_t0_indiv + sigma^2),
      R_baseline_within_season =
        `sd_rings_comb:season__baseline_Intercept`^2 /
        (`sd_rings_comb:season__baseline_Intercept`^2 + sigma^2),
      R_t0_within_season =
        `sd_rings_comb:season__t0_Intercept`^2 /
        (`sd_rings_comb:season__t0_Intercept`^2 + sigma^2),
      var_baseline_rings_comb = sd_rings_comb__baseline_Intercept^2,
      var_t0_rings_comb = sd_rings_comb__t0_Intercept^2,
      cor_baseline_t0 = cor_rings_comb__baseline_Intercept__t0_Intercept,
      resid_var = sigma^2
    )
}

# Keep the HTML diagnostics compact by reducing each fitted model to a few convergence checks.
model_diagnostics_table <- function(model_list) {
  purrr::imap_dfr(model_list, function(model_obj, model_name) {
    draw_summary <- posterior::summarise_draws(
      posterior::as_draws_array(model_obj$fitted_model),
      "rhat",
      "ess_bulk",
      "ess_tail"
    )

    tibble(
      model = model_name,
      max_rhat = round(max(draw_summary$rhat, na.rm = TRUE), 3),
      n_rhat_over_1_01 = sum(draw_summary$rhat > 1.01, na.rm = TRUE),
      min_bulk_ess = floor(min(draw_summary$ess_bulk, na.rm = TRUE)),
      min_tail_ess = floor(min(draw_summary$ess_tail, na.rm = TRUE))
    )
  })
}

# Use the same simple table style across the Results section.
display_result_table <- function(tbl) {
  tbl %>%
    select(parameter, median_CI) %>%
    gt() %>%
    cols_label(
      parameter = "Parameter",
      median_CI = gt::md("Posterior median [95% CrI]")
    ) %>%
    cols_align(align = "left", columns = everything())
}

display_consolidated_table <- function(tbl) {
  count_model_individuals <- function(obj_name, fallback) {
    if (exists(obj_name, inherits = TRUE)) {
      obj <- get(obj_name, inherits = TRUE)
      if (is.list(obj) && "data" %in% names(obj) && "rings_comb" %in% names(obj$data)) {
        return(dplyr::n_distinct(obj$data$rings_comb))
      }
    }

    fallback
  }

  format_num <- function(x, digits = 2) {
    out <- format(round(x, digits), nsmall = digits, trim = TRUE, scientific = FALSE)
    out <- sub("(?<=\\d)0+$", "", out, perl = TRUE)
    out <- sub("\\.$", "", out)
    out <- sub("^-0$", "0", out)
    out
  }

  format_date_median <- function(x, origin_year = 2021) {
    sub("^0", "", format(shifted_julian_to_date(x, origin_year), "%d-%b"))
  }

  format_date_ci <- function(lo, hi, origin_year = 2021) {
    paste0(
      "[",
      sub("^0", "", format(shifted_julian_to_date(lo, origin_year), "%d %b")),
      ", ",
      sub("^0", "", format(shifted_julian_to_date(hi, origin_year), "%d %b")),
      "]"
    )
  }

  n_lookup <- c(
    "Sex-specific" = count_model_individuals("sex_mod", 100L),
    "Breeding phenology" = count_model_individuals("sex_all_3_breed_mod", 81L),
    "Age-class" = count_model_individuals("sex_age_class_mod", 30L),
    "Within-individual centering" = count_model_individuals("sex_age_avg_mod", 30L),
    "Migratory status" = count_model_individuals("sex_migratory_status_mod", 41L)
  )

  section_levels <- c(
    "Fixed effects on baseline breeding plumage score (β)",
    "Fixed effects on post-breeding molt midpoint (t0): sex, age, and migration strategy",
    "Fixed effects on post-breeding molt midpoint (t0): effect of +1 SD (within-season) delay in breeding phenology trait",
    "Derived fixed effects: post-breeding molt rate at t0",
    "Repeatability of molt traits (R)",
    "Model fit",
    "Variance components and residual structure"
  )

  display_tbl <- tbl %>%
    mutate(
      section = case_when(
        grepl("first breeding date|last breeding date|last nest date", parameter, ignore.case = TRUE) ~
          "Fixed effects on post-breeding molt midpoint (t0): effect of +1 SD (within-season) delay in breeding phenology trait",
        grepl("rate at inflection", parameter, ignore.case = TRUE) ~
          "Derived fixed effects: post-breeding molt rate at t0",
        type == "Repeatability" ~ "Repeatability of molt traits (R)",
        type == "Model-level goodness-of-fit" ~ "Model fit",
        type %in% c(
          "Between-individual variance (random effects)",
          "Random-effects correlation",
          "Residual (within-observation) variance"
        ) ~ "Variance components and residual structure",
        grepl("baseline alternate plumage score", parameter, ignore.case = TRUE) ~
          "Fixed effects on baseline breeding plumage score (β)",
        grepl("moult mid", parameter, ignore.case = TRUE) ~
          "Fixed effects on post-breeding molt midpoint (t0): sex, age, and migration strategy",
        TRUE ~ type
      ),
      row_label = case_when(
        grepl("^Baseline alternate plumage score.*females", parameter) ~
          "Intercept, females (reference; score)",
        grepl("^Sex effect on baseline alternate plumage score", parameter) ~
          "Sex effect (males – females; Δ score)",
        grepl("^Age-class effect on baseline alternate plumage score", parameter) ~
          "Age-class effect (AFY – FY; Δ score)",
        grepl("^Between-individual age effect on baseline alternate plumage score", parameter) ~
          "Between-individual age effect (Δ score)",
        grepl("^Within-individual age effect on baseline alternate plumage score", parameter) ~
          "Within-individual age effect (Δ score)",
        grepl("^Migratory-strategy effect on baseline alternate plumage score", parameter) ~
          "Migratory-strategy effect (Resident – Migrant; Δ score)",
        grepl("^Pre-basic moult midpoint.*females", parameter) ~
          "Intercept, females (reference; date)",
        grepl("^Sex effect on pre-basic moult mid", parameter) ~
          "Sex effect (males – females; Δ days)",
        grepl("^Age-class effect on pre-basic moult", parameter) ~
          "Age-class effect (AFY – FY; Δ days)",
        grepl("^Between-individual age effect on pre-basic moult midpoint", parameter) ~
          "Between-individual age effect (Δ days)",
        grepl("^Within-individual age effect on pre-basic moult midpoint", parameter) ~
          "Within-individual age effect (Δ days)",
        grepl("^Migratory-strategy effect on pre-basic moult midpoint", parameter) ~
          "Migratory-strategy effect (Resident – Migrant; Δ days)",
        grepl("first breeding date", parameter, ignore.case = TRUE) ~
          "First date observed breeding (Δ days)",
        grepl("last breeding date", parameter, ignore.case = TRUE) ~
          "Last date observed breeding (Δ days)",
        grepl("last nest date", parameter, ignore.case = TRUE) ~
          "Last nest initiation date (Δ days)",
        grepl("^Pre-basic moult rate at inflection.*females", parameter) ~
          "Intercept, females (reference; days·score⁻¹)",
        grepl("^Sex effect on pre-basic moult rate at inflection", parameter) ~
          "Sex effect (males – females; days·score⁻¹)",
        parameter == "Between-year repeatability of alternate plumage" ~
          "Between-year repeatability of β",
        parameter == "Within-year repeatability of alternate plumage" ~
          "Within-year repeatability of β",
        parameter == "Between-year repeatability of pre-basic moult mid-point date" ~
          "Between-year repeatability of t0",
        parameter == "Within-year repeatability of pre-basic moult mid-point date" ~
          "Within-year repeatability of t0",
        parameter == "Between-individual variance in baseline plumage score" ~
          "Between-individual variance in β",
        parameter == "Between-individual variance in pre-basic moult midpoint date" ~
          "Between-individual variance in t0",
        grepl("^Correlation between baseline alternate plumage score", parameter) ~
          "Correlation between β and t0",
        TRUE ~ parameter
      ),
      model = case_when(
        grepl("^Age-class effect", parameter) ~ "Age-class",
        grepl("^Between-individual age effect|^Within-individual age effect", parameter) ~
          "Within-individual centering",
        grepl("^Migratory-strategy effect", parameter) ~ "Migratory status",
        grepl("first breeding date|last breeding date|last nest date", parameter, ignore.case = TRUE) ~
          "Breeding phenology",
        TRUE ~ "Sex-specific"
      ),
      n_ind = as.integer(unname(n_lookup[model])),
      posterior_median = if_else(
        grepl("^Pre-basic moult midpoint.*females", parameter),
        format_date_median(estimate),
        format_num(estimate)
      ),
      credible_interval = if_else(
        grepl("^Pre-basic moult midpoint.*females", parameter),
        format_date_ci(ci_lower, ci_upper),
        paste0("[", format_num(ci_lower), ", ", format_num(ci_upper), "]")
      ),
      section = factor(section, levels = section_levels)
    ) %>%
    select(section, row_label, posterior_median, credible_interval, n_ind, model)

  display_tbl %>%
    gt::gt(groupname_col = "section", rowname_col = "row_label") %>%
    gt::tab_stubhead(label = "") %>%
    gt::cols_label(
      posterior_median = "Posterior median",
      credible_interval = "[95% CrI]",
      n_ind = gt::html("N<sub>ind</sub>"),
      model = "Model"
    ) %>%
    gt::cols_align(
      align = "center",
      columns = c(posterior_median, credible_interval, n_ind)
    ) %>%
    gt::cols_align(align = "left", columns = model) %>%
    gt::cols_width(
      gt::stub() ~ gt::px(360),
      posterior_median ~ gt::px(115),
      credible_interval ~ gt::px(150),
      n_ind ~ gt::px(60),
      model ~ gt::px(150)
    ) %>%
    gt::tab_style(
      style = gt::cell_text(weight = "bold"),
      locations = gt::cells_row_groups()
    ) %>%
    gt::tab_style(
      style = gt::cell_borders(sides = "top", color = "black", weight = gt::px(1)),
      locations = gt::cells_row_groups()
    ) %>%
    gt::tab_options(
      table.width = gt::pct(100),
      table.font.size = gt::px(12),
      data_row.padding = gt::px(3),
      row_group.padding = gt::px(4),
      source_notes.font.size = gt::px(11)
    ) %>%
    gt::tab_source_note(
      source_note = gt::md(
        "Note: Δ denotes an additive difference relative to the reference category (for categorical predictors) or the expected change in the response per unit change in the predictor (for continuous covariates)."
      )
    )
}
```

```{r results-load-models, echo=FALSE}
# Load each saved model once so the visible chunks can stay short and readable.
sex_mod <- readRDS(here("out/sex_mod.rds"))
sex_all_3_breed_mod <- readRDS(here("out/sex_all_3_breed_mod.rds"))
sex_age_class_mod <- readRDS(here("out/sex_age_class_mod.rds"))
sex_age_avg_mod <- readRDS(here("out/sex_age_avg_mod.rds"))
sex_migratory_status_mod <- readRDS(here("out/sex_migratory_status_mod.rds"))

results_models <- list(
  "Sex-only moult model" = sex_mod,
  "Sex + breeding phenology model" = sex_all_3_breed_mod,
  "Sex + age-class model" = sex_age_class_mod,
  "Sex + continuous-age model" = sex_age_avg_mod,
  "Sex + migratory-status model" = sex_migratory_status_mod
)

kfc_age_class <- readRDS(here("out/kfc_age_class.rds"))
kfc_age_avg <- readRDS(here("out/kfc_age_avg.rds"))
```

#### Diagnostics
Report compact convergence summaries for each fitted model rather than printing full `brms` output
```{r results-diagnostics, echo=FALSE}
diagnostics_table <- model_diagnostics_table(results_models)

diagnostics_table %>%
  gt() %>%
  cols_label(
    model = "Model",
    max_rhat = "Max R-hat",
    n_rhat_over_1_01 = "Parameters with R-hat > 1.01",
    min_bulk_ess = "Min bulk ESS",
    min_tail_ess = "Min tail ESS"
  )
```

Compare the two age parameterisations using the saved 10-fold cross-validation objects.
```{r results-age-model-comparison}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code for the 10-fold cross-validation"
age_model_comparison <- loo_compare(kfc_age_class, kfc_age_avg) %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("model") %>%
  mutate(
    model = recode(
      model,
      kfc_age_class = "Age-class model",
      kfc_age_avg = "Continuous-age model"
    ),
    across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x, 2))
  )

age_model_comparison %>%
  gt() %>%
  cols_label(
    model = "Model",
    elpd_diff = "elpd diff",
    se_diff = "SE diff"
  )
```

### Results: Sex-specific model
We use the sex-only model as the main reference because it uses the full dataset. It shows clear sex differences in baseline ornament expression, little evidence for a sex difference in molt midpoint, and substantial individual consistency in both expression and timing.

```{r results-sex-model, echo=FALSE}
# Start from the saved posterior draws so this chunk reproduces the reported table without refitting.
sex_post <- as_draws_df(sex_mod$fitted_model)

# Use season-weighted centering/scaling constants to convert the model's t0 estimate back to days.
date_scale <- weighted_date_scaling(sex_mod$data)
mean_date_sd <- date_scale$weighted_sd
mean_date_mean <- date_scale$weighted_mean

# Derive the biological quantities reported in the consolidated table.
sex_post <- sex_post %>%
  mutate(
    baseline_sexF = b_baseline_Intercept,
    baseline_sexM = b_baseline_Intercept + b_baseline_sexM,
    baseline_sex_diff = b_baseline_sexM,
    t0_sexF = b_t0_Intercept * mean_date_sd + mean_date_mean,
    t0_sex_diff = b_t0_sexM * mean_date_sd,
    rate_days_score_sexF = 1 / (((baseline_sexF - 1) * b_k_Intercept) / (4 * mean_date_sd)),
    rate_days_score_sexM = 1 / (((baseline_sexM - 1) * b_k_Intercept) / (4 * mean_date_sd)),
    rate_days_score_diff = rate_days_score_sexM - rate_days_score_sexF
  ) %>%
  add_repeatability_draws()

# Bayesian R² is already summarised, so convert it directly into table rows.
sex_r2 <- summarise_bayes_r2(sex_mod$fitted_model)

sex_mod_summary_table <- bind_rows(
  summary_row(
    "Baseline alternate plumage score (β), females (reference)",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Full alternate plumage score (beta)",
    sex_post$baseline_sexF
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Sex effect on baseline alternate plumage score (β): males − females",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Full alternate plumage score (beta)",
    sex_post$baseline_sex_diff
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Pre-basic moult midpoint (t0), females (reference)",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Pre-basic moult mid-point date (t0)",
    sex_post$t0_sexF,
    formatter = format_shifted_julian_interval
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Sex effect on pre-basic moult midpoint (t0): males − females",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Pre-basic moult mid-point date (t0)",
    sex_post$t0_sex_diff
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Pre-basic moult rate at inflection (days·score⁻¹), females (reference)",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Moult rate (days·score⁻¹, k)",
    sex_post$rate_days_score_sexF
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Sex effect on pre-basic moult rate at inflection (days·score⁻¹): males − females",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Moult rate (days·score⁻¹, k)",
    sex_post$rate_days_score_diff
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Between-year repeatability of alternate plumage",
    "Repeatability",
    "",
    sex_post$R_baseline
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Within-year repeatability of alternate plumage",
    "Repeatability",
    "",
    sex_post$R_baseline_within_season
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Between-year repeatability of pre-basic moult mid-point date",
    "Repeatability",
    "",
    sex_post$R_t0
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Within-year repeatability of pre-basic moult mid-point date",
    "Repeatability",
    "",
    sex_post$R_t0_within_season
  ),
  summary_row_from_stats(
    "Marginal R²",
    "Model-level goodness-of-fit",
    "",
    sex_r2 %>% slice(1)
  ),
  summary_row_from_stats(
    "Conditional R²",
    "Model-level goodness-of-fit",
    "",
    sex_r2 %>% slice(2)
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Between-individual variance in baseline plumage score",
    "Between-individual variance (random effects)",
    "",
    sex_post$var_baseline_rings_comb
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Between-individual variance in pre-basic moult midpoint date",
    "Between-individual variance (random effects)",
    "",
    sex_post$var_t0_rings_comb
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Correlation between baseline alternate plumage score and pre-basic moult midpoint date",
    "Random-effects correlation",
    "",
    sex_post$cor_baseline_t0
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Residual variance",
    "Residual (within-observation) variance",
    "",
    sex_post$resid_var
  )
)

display_result_table(sex_mod_summary_table)
```

### Results: Breeding Phenology model
The effect of first breeding date, last breeding date, and last nest initiation date were all small on the day scale. Specifically, later breeding activity did not translate into a clear delay in the post-breeding molt midpoint.

```{r results-breeding-phenology-model, echo=FALSE}
breed_post <- as_draws_df(sex_all_3_breed_mod$fitted_model)

# Use one record per bird-season when recovering the original-day SD for each breeding predictor.
breed_predictors <- sex_all_3_breed_mod$data %>%
  distinct(
    rings_comb, sex, season,
    first_breeding_date_J_snum,
    last_breeding_date_J_snum,
    last_nest_first_obs_J_snum
  )

first_breed_sd <- weighted_predictor_sd(breed_predictors, "first_breeding_date_J_snum")
last_breed_sd <- weighted_predictor_sd(breed_predictors, "last_breeding_date_J_snum")
last_lay_sd <- weighted_predictor_sd(breed_predictors, "last_nest_first_obs_J_snum")

sex_all_3_summary_table <- bind_rows(
  summary_row(
    "Δ pre-basic moult midpoint (t0; days) per +1 SD (within-season) delay in first breeding date",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Breeding phenology",
    breed_post$b_t0_first_breeding_date_J_snum * first_breed_sd
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Δ pre-basic moult midpoint (t0; days) per +1 SD (within-season) delay in last breeding date",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Breeding phenology",
    breed_post$b_t0_last_breeding_date_J_snum * last_breed_sd
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Δ pre-basic moult midpoint (t0; days) per +1 SD (within-season) delay in last nest date",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Breeding phenology",
    breed_post$b_t0_last_nest_first_obs_J_snum * last_lay_sd
  )
)

display_result_table(sex_all_3_summary_table)
```

### Results: Age Class model
The age-class model showed a clear contrast between first-year and after-first-year birds. Older birds tended to have slightly higher baseline scores and later molt midpoints.

```{r results-age-class-model, echo=FALSE}
age_class_post <- as_draws_df(sex_age_class_mod$fitted_model)

# Convert the age-class t0 effect from scaled units back to days.
date_scale <- weighted_date_scaling(sex_age_class_mod$data)
mean_date_sd <- date_scale$weighted_sd

sex_age_class_mod_summary_table <- bind_rows(
  summary_row(
    "Age-class effect on baseline alternate plumage score (β): AFY − FY",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Full alternate plumage score (beta)",
    age_class_post$b_baseline_age_classAFY
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Age-class effect on pre-basic moult mid-point date (t0): AFY − FY",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Pre-basic moult mid-point date (t0)",
    age_class_post$b_t0_age_classAFY * mean_date_sd
  )
)

display_result_table(sex_age_class_mod_summary_table)
```

### Results: Continuous Age model
Treating age as a continuous trait gave the same broad result as the age class model, but demonstrated that the age effect extended beyond the transition from the first-year cycle. Older birds tended to show higher baseline scores and later molt midpoints, with evidence for both between-individual and within-individual age effects.

```{r results-age-model, echo=FALSE}
age_post <- as_draws_df(sex_age_avg_mod$fitted_model)

# Convert the t0 age effects from scaled units back to days per year.
date_scale <- weighted_date_scaling(sex_age_avg_mod$data)
mean_date_sd <- date_scale$weighted_sd

sex_age_avg_mod_summary_table <- bind_rows(
  summary_row(
    "Between-individual age effect on baseline alternate plumage score (β)",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Full alternate plumage score (beta)",
    age_post$b_baseline_avg_age
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Within-individual age effect on baseline alternate plumage score (β)",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Full alternate plumage score (beta)",
    age_post$b_baseline_age_dev
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Between-individual age effect on pre-basic moult midpoint (t0)",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Pre-basic moult mid-point date (t0)",
    age_post$b_t0_avg_age * mean_date_sd
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Within-individual age effect on pre-basic moult midpoint (t0)",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Pre-basic moult mid-point date (t0)",
    age_post$b_t0_age_dev * mean_date_sd
  )
)

display_result_table(sex_age_avg_mod_summary_table)
```

### Results: Migratory Strategy model
Residents and migrants showed similar molt timing. Residents tended to have slightly higher baseline scores, but the overlap was broad and the timing contrast was weak.

```{r results-migratory-model, echo=FALSE}
# The consolidated table only reports the migratory-status contrasts, so keep this chunk to those rows.
migratory_post <- as_draws_df(sex_migratory_status_mod$fitted_model)

# Convert the migratory-status effect on t0 from scaled units back to days.
date_scale <- weighted_date_scaling(sex_migratory_status_mod$data)
mean_date_sd <- date_scale$weighted_sd

sex_migratory_status_mod_summary_table <- bind_rows(
  summary_row(
    "Migratory-strategy effect on baseline alternate plumage score (β): Resident − Migrant",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Full alternate plumage score (beta)",
    migratory_post$b_baseline_migratory_statusR
  ),
  summary_row(
    "Migratory-strategy effect on pre-basic moult midpoint (t0): Resident − Migrant",
    "Posterior estimates (state-specific means)",
    "Pre-basic moult mid-point date (t0)",
    migratory_post$b_t0_migratory_statusR * mean_date_sd
  )
)

display_result_table(sex_migratory_status_mod_summary_table)
```

### Table 1
Consolidated table of results

```{r results-consolidated-table, echo=FALSE}
# Assemble the final table in the same reporting order used in the manuscript text.
grand_summary_table <- bind_rows(
  sex_mod_summary_table %>% slice(1:2),
  sex_age_class_mod_summary_table %>% slice(1),
  sex_age_avg_mod_summary_table %>% slice(1:2),
  sex_migratory_status_mod_summary_table %>% slice(1),
  sex_mod_summary_table %>% slice(3:4),
  sex_age_class_mod_summary_table %>% slice(2),
  sex_age_avg_mod_summary_table %>% slice(3:4),
  sex_migratory_status_mod_summary_table %>% slice(2),
  sex_all_3_summary_table,
  sex_mod_summary_table %>% slice_tail(n = nrow(sex_mod_summary_table) - 4)
)

display_consolidated_table(grand_summary_table)
```

```{r results-export-table, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# Optional: export the final reported table exactly as displayed above.
grand_summary_table_export <- grand_summary_table %>%
  select(type, parameter_, parameter, median_CI) %>%
  rename(
    Category = type,
    Group = parameter_,
    Parameter = parameter,
    Estimate_95CI = median_CI
  )

library(writexl)
write_xlsx(
  grand_summary_table_export,
  here("out/grand_summary_table_final.xlsx")
)
```

## Figures
### Figure 2 - raw data overview

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code for figure 2"
breeding_data_all <- readRDS(here("data/breeding_KK_data.rds"))

#### breeding histograms
# 1) Get per-season centering/scaling constants from the ORIGINAL (unscaled) shifted-Julian variables
stats <- breeding_data_all %>%
  group_by(season) %>%
  summarise(
    mu_last  = mean(last_breeding_date_J, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_last  = sd(last_breeding_date_J,  na.rm = TRUE),
    mu_first = mean(first_breeding_date_J, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_first = sd(first_breeding_date_J,  na.rm = TRUE),
    mu_nest  = mean(last_nest_first_obs_J, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_nest  = sd(last_nest_first_obs_J,  na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# 2) Join constants, invert transform, then convert shifted-Julian (1 = Jul 1) to a common calendar date
ref_start <- as.Date("2001-07-01")  # any non-leap year works well

out_dates <- dat_breeding %>%
  distinct(rings_comb, season,
           last_breeding_date_J_snum,
           first_breeding_date_J_snum,
           last_nest_first_obs_J_snum) %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  left_join(stats, by = "season") %>%
  mutate(
    # If _snum is CENTER-ONLY (scale = 0): x = centered + mean
    last_J  = last_breeding_date_J_snum  + mu_last,
    first_J = first_breeding_date_J_snum + mu_first,
    nest_J  = last_nest_first_obs_J_snum + mu_nest,

    # Convert shifted-Julian to a common calendar date (same year for all seasons)
    last_date_common  = ref_start + round(last_J  - 1),
    first_date_common = ref_start + round(first_J - 1),
    nest_date_common  = ref_start + round(nest_J  - 1),

    # Optional: month-day labels (often what you want for plotting)
    last_md  = format(last_date_common,  "%b-%d"),
    first_md = format(first_date_common, "%b-%d"),
    nest_md  = format(nest_date_common,  "%b-%d")
  ) %>%
  select(rings_comb, season,
         last_J, first_J, nest_J,
         last_date_common, first_date_common, nest_date_common,
         last_md, first_md, nest_md)

out_dates_long <- out_dates %>%
  select(rings_comb, season, last_date_common, first_date_common, nest_date_common) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(last_date_common, first_date_common, nest_date_common),
    names_to = "phenology_trait",
    values_to = "date_common"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    phenology_trait = sub("_date_common$", "_date", phenology_trait),
    phenology_trait = sub("_common$", "", phenology_trait)
  )

# over lapping histograms
nesting_hist <- 
  ggplot(
      out_dates_long %>%
        filter(phenology_trait %in% c("first_date", "last_date")),
      aes(x = date_common, fill = phenology_trait)
    ) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 7, position = "identity", alpha = 0.55, colour = NA) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(family = "Lato"),
    axis.title.x = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    axis.title.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.margin = margin(0.2, 0.2, -15, 0.2)
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(position = "left", breaks = c(0, 10, 20), limits = c(0, 25)) +
  scale_x_date(
    date_labels = "%B", expand = c(0.01, 0.01),
    date_breaks = "2 month",
    limits = c(as.Date("2001-07-01"), as.Date("2002-05-01"))
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(first_date = "grey70", last_date = "grey30")
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = as.Date("2001-07-25"),
    y = 15,
    label = "First\nbreeding\ndates",
    fontface = "italic",
    family = "Lato",
    size = 3,
    hjust = 0.5,
    vjust = 0,
    colour = "grey30"
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = as.Date("2002-01-25"),
    y = 15,
    label = "Last\nbreeding\ndates",
    fontface = "italic",
    family = "Lato",
    size = 3,
    hjust = 0.5,
    vjust = 0,
    colour = "grey30"
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = as.Date("2002-03-01"),
    y = 20,
    label = "20",
    fontface = "italic",
    family = "Lato",
    size = 3,
    hjust = 0.5,
    vjust = 0,
    colour = "grey30"
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = as.Date("2002-03-01"),
    y = 10,
    label = "10",
    fontface = "italic",
    family = "Lato",
    size = 3,
    hjust = 0.5,
    vjust = 0,
    colour = "grey30"
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = as.Date("2002-03-01"),
    y = 0,
    label = "0",
    fontface = "italic",
    family = "Lato",
    size = 3,
    hjust = 0.5,
    vjust = 0,
    colour = "grey30"
  )

#### raw data and example individual trajectories
nests_data <- readRDS(here("data/nests_KK_data.rds"))

dates_for_plot <-
  data.frame(date_ = as.Date(c(as.Date("2021-07-01"):as.Date("2022-06-30"))),
             date_J = c(1:365))

# 1) Build a clean prediction grid for the two individuals across dates and seasons
newdata_ind <- tidyr::expand_grid(
  date_J_scaled = seq(
    from = min(sex_mod$data$date_J_scaled),
    to   = max(sex_mod$data$date_J_scaled),
    length.out = max(sex_mod$data$date_J) - min(sex_mod$data$date_J) + 1
  ),
  rings_comb = c("RRGO", "RGGL"),
  season     = levels(sex_mod$data$season)   # e.g., c("2021","2022","2023")
) %>%
  # assign sex per individual from your observed data (safer than expanding sex)
  left_join(
    sex_mod$data %>%
      distinct(rings_comb, sex) %>%
      filter(rings_comb %in% c("RRGO", "RGGL")),
    by = "rings_comb"
  ) %>%
  # if you want Julian date (unscaled) + calendar date for plotting, join your helper tables
  left_join(
    data.frame(
      date_J = seq(min(sex_mod$data$date_J), max(sex_mod$data$date_J), length.out =
                     max(sex_mod$data$date_J) - min(sex_mod$data$date_J) + 1),
      date_J_scaled = seq(min(sex_mod$data$date_J_scaled), max(sex_mod$data$date_J_scaled), length.out =
                            max(sex_mod$data$date_J) - min(sex_mod$data$date_J) + 1)
    ),
    by = "date_J_scaled"
  ) %>%
  left_join(dates_for_plot, by = "date_J")

# 2) Extract expected-value draws (includes group-level effects by default)
epred_draws_ind <- add_epred_draws(
  sex_mod$fitted_model,
  newdata = newdata_ind,
  ndraws = 50
)

photo_data <-
  dat_breeding %>%
  filter(filename %in% c("IMG_0053.JPG", "IMG_0117.JPG", "IMG_0146.JPG", "IMG_0722.JPG", "IMG_2065.JPG", "IMG_0411.JPG")) %>%
  select(rings_comb, sex, migratory_status, age_at_banding, age, score, date_J, date) %>%
  left_join(., dates_for_plot, by = "date_J") %>%
  mutate(score = as.numeric(score))

found_dates_all <- 
  nests_data %>% 
  filter(!is.na(found_date)) %>%
  mutate(found_date_J = as.integer(
    found_date - as.Date(paste0(year(add_with_rollback(found_date, -months(6))), "-07-01"))
  ) + 1)

found_dates_for_plot <-
  data.frame(date_ = as.Date(c(as.Date("2021-07-01"):as.Date("2022-06-30"))),
             found_date_J = c(1:365))

found_dates_all_ <-
  found_dates_all %>%
  left_join(., found_dates_for_plot, by = "found_date_J") %>%
  # select(-rings_comb) %>%
  distinct()

sex_score_plot <-
  ggplot() +
  # Add the faint box first
  geom_rect(aes(xmin = min(found_dates_all_$date_), xmax = max(found_dates_all_$date_),
                ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
            fill = "grey70", alpha = 0.2) +  # adjust fill and alpha as needed +
  geom_jitter(data = dat_breeding %>% 
                left_join(., dates_for_plot, by = "date_J") %>% 
                mutate(score = as.numeric(score)) %>% 
                filter(!is.na(sex)),
              aes(x = date_, y = score), fill = "black",
              shape = 16, size = 3, alpha = 0.2,
              height = 0.15, width = 0) +
  geom_line(data = epred_draws_ind %>% 
                filter((rings_comb == "RGGL" & season == 2023) | 
                         (rings_comb == "RRGO" & season == 2022)), 
            aes(x = date_, y = .epred, color = sex,
                group = interaction(rings_comb, .draw, season)), alpha = 0.1) +
  geom_point(data = dat_breeding %>% 
                left_join(., dates_for_plot, by = "date_J") %>% 
                mutate(score = as.numeric(score)) %>% 
                filter(!is.na(sex)) %>% 
                filter((rings_comb == "RGGL" & season == 2023) | 
                         (rings_comb == "RRGO" & season == 2022)), 
             aes(x = date_, y = score, fill = sex),
             shape = 21, color = "white", size = 2, stroke = 0.5) +
  geom_point(data = photo_data %>% filter(score %in% c(5, 6)),
             aes(x = date_, y = score, fill = sex),
             shape = 24, color = "white", size = 4, stroke = 1) +
  geom_point(data = photo_data %>% filter(score %in% c(4)),
             aes(x = date_, y = score, fill = sex),
             shape = 22, color = "white", size = 4, stroke = 1) +
  geom_point(data = photo_data %>% filter(score %in% c(2)),
             aes(x = date_, y = score, fill = sex),
             shape = 25, color = "white", size = 4, stroke = 1) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(family = "Lato"),
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        axis.title.y = element_text(size = 11, colour = "black"),
        axis.text.y = element_text(size = 9, colour = "grey30"),
        # plot.margin = unit(c(0,5.5,5.5,0), "cm"),
        panel.spacing = unit(1, "cm"),
        # panel.border = element_rect(size = 0.25, colour = "grey70", fill = 'transparent'),
        panel.background = element_rect(fill = 'transparent', color = NA),
        plot.background = element_rect(fill = 'transparent', color = NA),
        strip.background = element_rect(fill = 'grey80', color = NA),
```
